content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies with Odd Female Characters

**Score:** 55
**Body:** So I just finished watching the Secretary and I loved it. Of course, I want to watch movies similar to it. Not necessarily about BDSM (although I won’t object) but more about finding that someone who sees you and gets you, despite your oddities. Or even just a movie about odd, off-putting female characters. Some movies that I’ve watch that I’ve enjoyed and fit this vibe (in my opinion) are May, Dinner in America, and Carrie. I would love some recommendations similar to Secretary or the other movies I’ve listed!!

Edit: I just want to say thank you for all the movie recommendations!! I look forward to watching them :)

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I wouldn't say she's "off-putting", but definitely odd (in a nice way!)

Amèlie (2001)
  - Another good one that's a sort of flipside of Audrey Tautou is He Loves Me, He Loves Me Not. She's a different sort of odd in it.
2. Heavenly Creatures
3. Benny and Joon. Exactly what you describe starring Johnny Depp
4. Muriel’s Wedding
5. Yes! Weird girl movies are a fave genre! I was gonna suggest May! But, some others you may enjoy are:

American Mary, Jennifer's Body, Heathers, The Love Witch, The Black Swan, Promising Young Women, Drop Dead  Goregous, The Virgin Suicides, Marie Antionette
  - I love drop dead gorgeous! And you are the first other person I have heard mention it, so you are automatically cool
6. Harold and Maude
7. Ghost World!
8. I Don't Feel at Home in this World Anymore  


Baise Moi



Primordial  


Strangers with Candy (the series is far superior to the movie imo, but the movie is good too)

## 2. Scariest depictions of hell in Cinema?

**Score:** 39
**Body:** Was wondering what some of the more scary/eerie depictions of hell have been? The depictions from Constantine, The Mummy Returns, and Hellboy (2019) as being examples I can think of. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What Dreams May Come
2. Event Horizon has gotta be up there
  - Came here to say this. Can’t think of a better answer.
  - I've seen that movie like three times. Every time I think "That wasn't so bad. Why don't I watch that more often?" 


Then come the "dreams"...
3. Baskin (turkish horror film). It makes you feel dirty.
4. Talk to me was pretty nightmarish.
5. Jacob's ladder
6. bill & Ted
7. The Frighteners when they get dragged down to hell at the end. The worms are nightmarish.
8. As Above So Below

## 3. Native American movies

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I’m not a fan of westerns, but I really enjoy movies that show the Native American culture. I’m currently watching Killers of the Flower Moon!

Some other favorites:

Prey (2022)

The Last of the Mohicans (1992)

Dances with Wolves (1990)

I also enjoyed the TV series Dexter: New Blood and True Detective: Night Country, as they both had Native American police officers solving crimes.

What else do ya got?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Smoke Signals 
  - Hey Victorrr!

Also Dance Me Outside
2. Reservation Dogs on Hulu. Thank me later.
3. For TV, Dark Winds. So damn good.
4. ***Thunderheart.***
5. WIndwalker (1981) is about Cheyenne and Crow in pre-colonial North America.

Little Big Man (1970): A white child is raised by the Cheyenne and is torn between two worlds throughout the course of a long and eventful life.
6. Dark Winds (TV), Reservation Dogs (TV ), Blood Quantum, Wind River
7. Antanarjuat is great. It’s Inuit, so not technically “native” or indigenous but close enough!
8. Clearcut (1991)
9. As someone who lives next to the oldest continually inhabited Native American pueblo community in North America, I highly recommend Reservation Dogs, Smoke Signals, and Dark Winds, as they are excellent AND made largely by and with actual Native American creators, actors, writers, and artists. 

Anything made before around 2010 is much less likely to be a respectful and informed representation of the actual people, and the various tribal cultures and cultural identity across the continent. 

There are exceptions, but if you love this content because the culture and the people speak to something in you, it would be a good idea to research each movie/show a little bit before watching, just to see whether the Native community was involved in/okay with the creation and representation. 

Enjoy!

## 4. What do you propose as an excellent movie to watch tonight with my girlfriend?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** An excellent movie in front of which you had a very good time, We want something funny or with a cool story, by way of comparison we liked : Catch me if you can, Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind, Forest Gump, Rango, Snatch, Seven, Porco Rosso etc 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My Cousin Vinny
  - This and What About Bob are my all time favorite comedies
2. Big Fish
3. True Romance
4. Grosse Pointe Blank (1997)
5. Princess Bride is a great date night movie
6. North By Northwest

May be a bit older a film than you’re used to, but snappy dialogue, intrigue, action from the get-go.
7. Almost Famous
8. If you enjoyed Eternal Sunshine, you would love Charlie Kaufman's other movies.

Being John Malkovich is a fun one. It's a bit of a slow start, but once it reaches it's point, it's a wild ride. He answers one of the most obvious questions in a clever, hilarious way.

I'm Thinking of Ending Things and Synecdoche New York are a couple of his "wtf is going on" movies that I absolutely loved.

I also recommend looking into Ari Aster films. Beau is Afraid should be on your list.

Swiss Army Man is hilarious if you're into over-the-top slapstick humor. Go in blind on this one.
9. Okay? Going off the vibes of good time. Funny and cool, i'm going to suggest

A fish called wanda

Get shorty

murder by death

Singing in the rain

Charade

Oliver

Repo man nineteen eighty four

Some like it hot

## 5. Life-changing movie recommendation

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Hello, I’m planning to watch good movies. Recommend me one that change your life after watching it. Sometimes I get lost searching for good movies and I think cinema sometimes it’s more a business than a real history or meaning behind the scenes. One that change the way I see life was interstellar and also Oppenheimer. Just shoot what you have, thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Suggested a lot, but I like Gattaca. It’s sci fi that focuses on its themes more than anything else. Cheers me up every time I watch it.
2. About Time could be one for you. It’s a good movie too.
  - About Time is a beautiful film that makes you appreciate the time you have with those you love, without being overly sentimental or a genuine tearjerker.
3. Step Brothers.

>"When I was a kid, I always wanted to be a dinosaur, a Tyrannosaurus Rex... I made my arms short, and I roamed the backyard, and I chased the neighborhood cats, and I growled and I roared... And one day my dad said, 'Bobby, you're 17, it's time to throw childish things aside.'... He said, 'Stop being a dinosaur and get a job.'... But you know, I thought to myself, I'll go to medical school, I'll practice for a little while, and then I'll come back to it. But I forgot how to do it... Don't lose your dinosaur!"

Hit me like a ton of bricks. I lost my dinosaur. 🥺
4. To Kill A Mockingbird had a huge impact on me, I can’t remember how old I was when I first saw it but I think I was young.
5. Not a movie but the whole of Twin Peaks
6. What Dreams May Come. The book is even better if course
7. 2001: A Space Odyssey deeply affected my outlook on life.
8. No movie has changed my life. But I recommend Apollo 13 -Tom Hanks as astronaut Jim Lovell-- simply because it shows a space crew in mortal danger for about 2 straight hours and it really happened (historical). Nice perspective just on a huge distance between Earth and the moon too, which is cool.
9. Tremors

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. I think this is the most gangsta line from a movie that I can remember…

**Score:** 3237
**Body:** Watchmen 2011

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh. Johnny, I apologize; I forgot you were there.


You may go now.
  - I really like, "Why, Johnny Ringo, you look like somebody just walked over your grave." (Because he actually was buried there after the duel if I remember correctly)

Honourable mention to "Not me, Im in my prime"
2. > "He's so drunk, he's probably seeing 2 of you!"

> "Well I've got 2 guns, one for each of ya"

Val Kilmer as Doc Holiday in Tombstone. Dude somehow makes a man half dead with tuberculosis one of the most badass gun slingers of all time.
3. “God says to forgive”
“Forgiveness is between them and God.
I’m here to set the meeting”
  - "Creaay's art is death; and he's about to paint his masterpiece."
4. Idk if it's "gangsta" or not but William Munny's lines at the end of Unforgiven always resonate with me:

Bill: you just shot an unarmed man!

Munny: any man that decorates his saloon with my dead friend should've armed himself.

and

Munny: All right, I'm coming out. Any man I see out there, I'm gonna shoot him. Any sumbitch takes a shot at me, I'm not only gonna kill him, but I'm gonna kill his wife, all his friends, and burn his damn house down.
  - Deserve’s got nothin to do with it.
5. My dad hates watching movies but this was on and he walked by as Rorschach said this line and he stops and says "Oooooh that sounded badass."
6. Did you really think you could kill me? I have walked across the surface of the sun. I’ve witnessed events so tiny and so fast they can hardly be said to have occurred at all. And you? You’re just a man. Earths smartest man poses no more threat to me than does its smartest termite
  - Such a great line.

I know Ozymandias cares most about executing his plan, but that’s still gotta hurt his ego a bit

## 2. One of my favorite scenes in Joker (2019) is the moment between Arthur and Gary, where Gary is unable to open the door. It creates a very different kind of humor in an otherwise disturbing situation.

**Score:** 1712

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It was very harrowing to watch, because I thought at any moment Arthur would snap and kill him too
  - Same, such a tense scene. 

Then Arthur even fucks with him, Which was kinda funny, but I felt bad for the guy.
2. That's why it pays to be nice.
  - "Thanks for the candy."
    - Here's some pens for your crazy pocket
3. I think it's hilarious and horrifying at the same time.  Because he has to ask Arthur for help, but asking only brings attention to himself.  It is the highlight of the film because of how absolutely horrible it is.
4. Tbf he did kill Errol Childress who was the serial killer in season 1 of true detective so he did a good thing in retrospect.
  - THE YELLOW KING IS DEAD
  - You in Carcosa now
    - “I just need you to stop saying weird shit.”

## 3. What were some of the biggest miscasts in Hollywood history according to you? I'll go first:

**Score:** 887
**Body:** Tom Holland as Nathan Drake in Uncharted (2022).

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The entire cast of Borderlands
  - Jack Black’s skills have really been put to such lazy use recently in the last like five years. 

He was fantastic in the Jumanji Reboots though.
2. John Wayne as Genghis Khan
3. Bill Skarsgård as Eric Draven.

In his defense, the entire film shouldn’t have been made.
4. Jesse Eisenberg as Lex Luthor in Batman VS Superman . Just felt OFF to me, it didn't work. And his line deliveries, sounded bad..
  - I don't know why modern adaptations of Lex Luthor are "nasal toned incredibly small weenie" but I am not super here for it.
    - Because the modern day equivalents to Lex are all tech bros

We live in the lamest reality
5. Tom Holland as Nathan Drake was one thing, especially when Nathan Fillion had already done the role so much justice in a single fifteen minute short film, but Marky Mark as fucking Sully is arguably the bigger crime here. 

Like, these aren’t even the same characters at that point, just make your own goddamn movie and don’t ruin what could have been 😭
  - Wahlberg as Sully is 100% a bigger miscast. Holland was bad but not at the same level of terrible.
6. Jared Leto as joker

## 4. Amanda Seyfried Says Winning an Oscar Is Not Important: ‘Do You Remember Who Won in the Past 10 Years?’

**Score:** 452

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Without reading the article I do think it had more importance before streaming because having an Academy Award winning/nominated actor/actress was a selling point for the movie. I think its less important now for viewers as they barely tune into the awards show. That said for someone in the film industry to be recognised by their peers and awarded the most prestigious award for film is a huge accomplishment.
  - What if I told you that the academy awards will be broadcast on YouTube soon? Probably hosted by Mr Beast or something
  - It’s just too Hollywood politics. The fact Shakespeare in love won over saving private ryan just shows how much it’s not a meritocracy
2. I can't tell you who won last year. Self-serving industry award shows disinterest me.
3. She says nominations and longevity are just as necessary for being hired. Headline is clickbait. She hasn’t won the Oscar, altho she would love it if she was, but she’s happy with her career without it.
4. She was so phenomenal in The Crowded Room.
  - If it was so crowded then how did you even see her?
5. Karen, you can’t just tell people their trophies are forgettable
6. Emma stone and um some others that seem to regret it or something.  Oh and that one chick who everyone was like who and what movie, like 5 people saw that movie how the hell did she win.
7. Read the article. I can’t believe we haven’t learned that by now. Headline is misleading.

## 5. I just finished this curious how it hit for everyone

**Score:** 257
**Body:** Uhhh? What? Also I do have a question for the end which if someone has seen it and can answer let me know.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Loved it. Plemons and Stone are now my favourite actors of the new generation after this one
  - Good call, Plemons has that deadpan "I’m 100% convinced" energy and and Stone sells the CEO vibe so hard that you’re never sure if she’s just ruthless… or actually not human. Ohh it was really good.
  - The actor that played Don... This was his very first movie. Dude killed it!
  - Hell yes, Plemons has been holding down that spot for me since Friday Night Lights and just crushing it consistently since.
2. The ending left me feeling flat
  - i c what u did there
  - Burst my bubble.
3. Top 5 movie of the year for me personally
  - It's #1 for me. Just adored it. I think it might even be my favorite Lanthimos movie, and that's saying something for me. Jesse Plemmoms is a superstar.
4. For those who don't know, it was a remake of a 2003 South Korean movie titled Save The Green Planet. I was honestly pretty lackluster on both. I watched a lot of Korean cinema for a while, and while I appreciated the originality of the original version, even then I thought there were more engaging things going on in Korean cinema. I appreciate both the efforts, but it's really just not my genre.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. “Rewatched Inception and realised the ‘real’ world has more dream logic than the dreams.”

**Score:** 112
**Body:** On rewatch, the dream layers feel rigid and rule-bound, while the “real” world is full of dreamlike shortcuts — Saito’s near-magical influence, Cobb’s instant clearance at the end, the children always appearing in the same pose, and emotional logic overriding realism.
It made me wonder whether Nolan intentionally made the real world operate with dream logic, while the dreams feel structured to misdirect the audience.
How do you read this contrast?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. As clearly intentional. He even has Mal explicitly point out said dream logic, “Think about it Cobb (“head” in Middle English*), shadowy conspiracies… unable to board a flight home…which is the dream?” The narrative itself is shaped like the Escher “paradox” steps the architect designs. The shape is always the best part of a Nolan project. 
——
Spoiler: Notice the totem at the end isn’t even Cobb’s. Whose dream are we in? 😉
  - I like this take. The way Mal calls out Cobb’s “dream logic” really makes the real-world layer feel unreliable. It’s interesting how Nolan makes the dream layers strict and rule-bound, but the “real” world is where logic bends the most.
  - I always liked the idea that Mal is in his dream because he forgot how to wake up, and is trying to just kill him in the dream to jolt him awake in the real world
  - Isn't it Mal's totem that he had started using and been using for years?
    - Yeah, I don't get why people always point that out. Totems aren't magically bound to their owner, all that matters is that nobody else knows how they behave.
      - Doesn't everybody know how the top behaves in the real world versus a dream world though?
        - To be a little more specific than the person you're replying to: it's not that it doesn't _behave_ in the same way, it's that someone creating it wouldn't be able to get the feel exactly right. Spinning tops always spin, so that part is easy to recreate, but getting the weighting, the balance, etc, exactly correct without being intimately familiar with the spinning top would be incredibly difficult.
  - As a speaker, I'm not sure Cobb has any relation to 'head' in Irish, if that's what you mean?
2. There's a really great piece (unfortunately by Devin Faraci) that talks about this

>Every single moment of Inception is a dream. I think that in a couple of years this will become the accepted reading of the film, and differing interpretations will have to be skillfully argued to be even remotely considered. The film makes this clear, and it never holds back the truth from audiences. Some find this idea to be narratively repugnant, since they think that a movie where everything is a dream is a movie without stakes, a movie where the audience is wasting their time.


>Except that this is exactly what Nolan is arguing against. The film is a metaphor for the way that Nolan as a director works, and what he’s ultimately saying is that the catharsis found in a dream is as real as the catharsis found in a movie is as real as the catharsis found in life. Inception is about making movies, and cinema is the shared dream that truly interests the director.


>I believe that Inception is a dream to the point where even the dream-sharing stuff is a dream. Dom Cobb isn’t an extractor. He can’t go into other people’s dreams. He isn’t on the run from the Cobol Corporation. At one point he tells himself this, through the voice of Mal, who is a projection of his own subconscious. She asks him how real he thinks his world is, where he’s being chased across the globe by faceless corporate goons. 

>She asks him that in a scene that we all know is a dream, but Inception lets us in on this elsewhere. Michael Caine’s character implores Cobb to return to reality, to wake up. During the chase in Mombasa, Cobb tries to escape down an alleyway, and the two buildings between which he’s running begin closing in on him – a classic anxiety dream moment. When he finally pulls himself free he finds Ken Watanabe’s character waiting for him, against all logic. Except dream logic.

https://chud.com/24477/never-wake-up-the-meaning-and-secret-of-inception/
3. The dreams have fantastical and stylized elements to them. They aren't overly surreal, but they aren't totally realistic either. The "real" world is similarly stylized and fantastical. (Machines to let you enter people's dreams exist on that "reality"). That real-world layer has farfetched elements like Cobb being pursued by shadowy agents. But it never breaks into the truly fanatastical. There's no folding cities or zero G in those parts of the film. These far-fetched elements in the real world add to the central ambiguities the film focuses on. (Mal even points them out directly at one point). That ambiguity would be diluted if the dreams in the second half of the film leaned too heavily into surreal imagery. When Eames conjures a big gun, he does it out of the frame because Nolan didn't want us to see that gun materialising in his hands because he was working to make those dreams grounded despite the many fantastical elements. You might argue it would make no difference to actually see Tom Hardy morphing into Tom Berenger because the end result is the same. But Nolan knew the impact would be different.

## 2. Why the Common Criticisms of Whiplash Miss the Point

**Score:** 65
**Body:** I really hate it when musicians and drummers criticize Whiplash for reasons like: “it’s unrealistic,” “it gives people the wrong idea about what playing in a big band is like,” “practicing to the point of pain or anger won’t help you improve,” “no drummer puts an ice bucket next to them while practicing,” or “the film doesn’t show the joy of playing music with others.”

When people say that, it honestly feels like they watched a completely different movie than I did.

Fletcher and Andrew are two lunatics. The entire film reinforces that at every possible opportunity. Whiplash never claims that this is the only way to achieve greatness. What it shows is that these two characters are convinced it is and we experience the whole story from their perspective. And as we’ve already established that these are deeply crazy people.

It’s like watching The Wolf of Wall Street and complaining that it doesn’t present a realistic or healthy portrayal of Wall Street. Yes exactly. That’s the point. And that’s why it’s a story worth telling as a film.

If there is a criticism that actually makes sense, it’s the idea that someone could abuse students this openly in a music education institution without facing consequences. But even that becomes more believable when you think about the sheer number of insane abuse stories that have come out in recent years. Stuff like this can happen — especially when someone like Fletcher is portrayed as a powerful figure with real influence and authority in that world

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'd argue the film does ultimately endorse the abusive methods to some degree. The core criticisms aren't saying "music conservatories aren't like this", it's that it fundamentally misunderstands how jazz actually works as an art form and undermines the story as a result. So it's a film about generic ambition and abuse that just happens to use jazz as dressing.

re: Wolf of Wall Street, this does understand finance culture even while showing its most depraved version.
  - Exactly. I loved Whiplash, and had a conversation with my brother’s friend shortly after it came out because he was headed to a Jazz concert and is a big fan.

He absolutely hated it lol, and said pretty much exactly this. 

But the funny thing too is that Chazelle then makes La La Land, and AGAIN has a subplot about Jazz - a “stick with traditions v embrace a pop evolution” thing - which Jazz aficionados AGAIN hated because that whole conversation was extremely old hat in the Jazz world and was long ago resolved with much more nuance than shown in the film. 

Now I still like both movies quite a bit, but I also entirely understand why people who love Jazz wouldn’t, given the director makes Jazz a core part of the films. 
  - This is true of nearly every job setting depicted in film.

- courtroom dramas aren’t realistic to what lawyers experience 
- sci fi / monster movies aren’t realistic to what science research is like
- Indiana Jones is not realistic archaeology 
- James Bond is not realistic espionage
- the battles in most historical epics and medieval fantasy films are most realistic battle tactics

Why draw a line at Jazz?
  - I disagree. I think it shows that victims of abuse come to rely on their abuser for validation, and Andrew _needs_ his own victimization in the end. He rejects his father and girlfriend and keeps looking for Fletcher’s approval. 

The film is an exercise in contrast. The “success” of Andrew’s final concert is stripped of satisfaction when he looks up and Fletcher again withholds approval, showing that the cycle of abuse will continue.
  - yeah the thing about wolf of wall street, if you read the book, it was actually even crazier than the movie. 

Whereas with whiplash, its like, a musician just wouldnt be in a band like that if the director was that insane lol.
2. I think you might be misinterpretating the objection people have. The objection that I hear most often is that Whiplash's premise and setting could easily be swapped out for a basketball player on a college team getting worked to the bone to become an amazing player by a toxic coach that demands perfection at the cost of literal blood sweat and tears, and the premise would remain intact. The setting of a conservatory and an abusive teacher who teaches in the way Fletcher does isn't accurate to the reality of most of not all jazz conservatories, but the setting itself is simply the vehicle the story uses to tell a story about obsession, perfectionism, and abuse. It's not specific to life in jazz conservatories; the message is a lot more general and widely applicable.
  - Hell most music is about learning how to feel something, not perfecting time. Maybe in prog rock and classical music, but when the height of Jazz music is a gentleman that changes beats on a whim, I really don't htink perfect timing is that necessary.
3. "Common criticisms" is doing a lot of heavy lifting.  

  
The problem with your Wolf of Wall Street comparison is that it's based on a true story.  Many of the story events bear some resemblance to what happened.  Scorsese often makes films based on real events: Raging Bull, The Aviator, Gangs of New York, Wolf of Wall Street, The Irishman, Killers of the Flower Moon, etc. The various forms of extremism isn't a bug, it's a feature of these people's real lives.    

That's less true in Whiplash
4. You have to qualify where the "Common Criticism" is coming from, then it makes more sense.

Adam Neely put it best in his (sorta) review: It's a great movie, no denying that! However, we as jazz musicians get represented extremely rarely in mainstream media, and the institution of higher jazz education at conservatories is pretty much entirely arcane. So when the only bit of mainstream meda in .... well, *ever*, that shows a glimpse into our world, represents it as filled with monomaniacal egotists, you can see why that makes people speak up.
5. It's two perfectionists with bloated egos who meet their match in each other.


It's not a depiction of the music scene in any way, just a depiction of obsession with perfection that can be equally self destructive and motivational. The actual drumming stuff is just the background - this conflict of two egos can be set in anything, be it sports, politics, music, anything. 

## 3. Is Oliver Stone still considered a major filmmaker?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Was talking movies with a family member and Oliver Stone's name came up as an example of a filmmaker whose reputation has fallen off.

As a millennial cinephile, I'm just old enough to remember when Oliver Stone making an epic movie about Alexander the Great was an event, something that casual movie fans were talking about.

But in the 2020s he's really not someone talked about that much in online film circles. Certainly not this subreddit. If he's been in the news or trending on social media in the last decade, it's been for his controversial politics or conspiratorial statements or sexual harassment, not for his movies.

Speaking of his movies, they did not do well on the most recent Sight & Sound poll. A handful of votes for 4 of his movies, none of which came close to cracking the top thousand.

In the 80s and 90s, he was one of the big names of American cinema who won multiple Oscars for movies that tackled big themes of American history and culture. If you asked someone 1990 to list major contemporary American filmmakers, his name would have definitely come up.

But what does his body of work look like in 2026?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The last narrative movie of his that I remember was Snowden, a pretty big standard biopic that was competently made and well acted but otherwise pretty drab. That film didn't so hot critically and financially, and it was ultimately the latest in a long line of flops. I don't know if he can't get work anymore or simply doesn't want to, but either way the fact that he's been in incognito for so long means he hasn't been able to keep his name in the conversation the way that someone like Spike Lee is able to.
  - Has he had a commercial or critical hit in the 21st century?
    - The only one I can think of is Savages. It made almost double the budget at the box office. But compared to things like Sicario, NCFOM, The Counselor, etc. it really did feel like your average cartel adjacent movie.
      - World Trade Center actually did better at the box office (both in box office and as percentage of budget) and Wall Street: Money Never Sleeps also made about double the budget, but with a bigger budget.

They're all in that range or worse though. So I reckon overall he didn't get close to making the budget of all his works in the 21st century back at the box office, though probably with DVD sales and now streaming overall they made a profit.

His last real success was Natural Born Killers , which made $110 million off a $34 million budget. And that was back in 1994.

Also, his three biopics after JFK (Nixon, W. and Snowden) combined made about 30 million less at the box office than their budgets (that was mostly Nixon though).
2. Stone had a good run for about a decade starting with Platoon and ending with Nixon. After that everything has had a pretty mixed reception critically. It’s hard to stay relevant when your body of work declines so much.
3. He's my favorite filmmaker of all-time but like most, he had his place in the sun. No filmmaker stays on top forever, especially as the type of movies he made (mid budget adult oriented dramas) are all but dead. He is also 79 years old and quite brutally honest that he does not have the energy nor passion to make films now, and is enjoying his retirement. He found his last film, Snowden, very hard and tedious to make and it flopped anyway. As Stone once said himself, he had a great ten year run of blank checks at the perfect time, which is more than most directors get, achieved his dreams and has a great body of work he's happy to leave behind. 

The double whammy of controversies over JFK and Natural Born Killers wore Stone down mentally (people forget that NBK was a years long legal battle over accused culpability in a murder case). Then the commercial flop of Nixon was another blow. He had some hits after that (Any Given Sunday, World Trade Center) but they were increasingly difficult as he aged. 

Interesting that you cite Spike Lee, because they are friends and also made/make the same type of movies, and Spike has continued to work, but it's been very hit and miss. He's been open about the disappointment of Da 5Bloods and lack of support from Netflix, for example. 

As for legacy, I'm 44 and exceedingly cynical about the "legacy" of any filmmaker in this age, where film history is on such a decline. I'm also old enough to remember how so many directors' reputations ebb and flow. 

Anecdotal: I remember doing an ice breaker at a work event which had a pop culture quiz. I mentioned something about Martin Scorsese and the others at the table (all 15 years or more younger) had no idea who I was talking about. I asked if any of them had seen Goodfellas? Blank stares. Killers of the Flower Moon? Blank stares. Wolf of Wall Street? Finally two people had seen it and called it "that old movie with Jonah Hill and Leo."

I think what Stone once said himself is correct, that he'll be evaluated and re-evaluated after he's dead if anyone still cares about film history, and if not he'll be dead anyway. Keep in mind that Stone had an entire life before becoming a director. He had unimaginable trauma and also achievements in the military, then he had a whole career as a top screenwriter before finally becoming a successful director. He's not going to grind until he dies like Spielberg or Scorsese.
4. I think his quality of work takes a dive in the 2000s. They also don't have the same polarizing subject matter. 

W. And Snowden are rather tame compared to JFK and Nixon. 

I also think his weird American history documentary which is just Russian propaganda also hurts him.
  - I think the whole Putin connection hurts him, reputation wise.

Especially since he made his name as an overtly political filmmaker. He's not a case where you can try to separate a body of work from its creator's politics, because they've always been intertwined.
5. His career arc seems very similar to that of Brian De Palma, although De Palma started a little earlier. De Palma declined after Mission to Mars in 2000. Stone declined after Alexander in 2004, and his films since then haven't gotten the same attention, except maybe W. They both had 30 years of solid filmmaking and keep making films, but those films don't have the same impact. It's hard to stay on top when there's always younger, hipper (and cheaper) filmmakers for Hollywood to turn to.
6. He’s essential to understanding American culture in the late 80s and the 90s. 

Platoon was the first major Vietnam movie made by a veteran of that war. Wall Street led to a huge uptick in people entering finance, and unwittingly distilled ‘greed is good’ as the Regan era dictum of American life. JFK launched a million new conspiracy theorists and foreshadowed our current ‘fake news’ moment. And Natural Born Killers captured the insane hysteria of pop culture and mass media, which still very much drives our culture today. 

Unfortunately, I think only a few of his movies are very good. He was hugely significant at the time, but his films don’t age well for sure.

## 4. Is a “sense of rhythm” really that important for directors and editors?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** So I’ve always heard that a strong sense of rhythm is absolutely crucial for directors and editors. It makes intuitive sense, and it’s hard to argue against, but I honestly can’t recall many films where the rhythm or pacing felt noticeably “off.” Are there examples where poor rhythm actually hurts the film? Curious to hear thoughts. Especially from pros. Thanks

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Well, compare Stalker or any middle-to-late 20th century film and compare it to, say Run Lola Run or one of the Bourne films or more contemporary films, and you will definitely see a difference in rhythm and pacing.

As long as we are watching successive images over time, the rate at which the image changes and the rhythm and flow of the cuts will be an essential aspect of how we experience visual media.

Spend time on TikTok and other social media, and you will see the tempo accelerated.

And don't forget that we are hearing as well. The rhythm is not just in the visuals - it's also in the dialog and the music and the sound design and their interplay with the visuals. The rhythm actually originates in the script.
  - I agree with you, but comparing Stalker, a slow burn art film, to fast paced (even for the time), more action-oriented genre films doesn't feel like the best comparison when they're all aiming for completely different audiences, regardless of whatever era they came out.
    - That is precisely the point. 

They each use rhythm *in different ways* to achieve *their particular aesthetic ends*.

Regardless of whether the pace is fast or slow or when a film was made, rhythm informs the experience of the film. 

Thus, a "sense of rhythm" is important.
      - Yeah your comparison made more sense once I re-read your original comment. Ty for clarifying
2. you won't see it a lot at a professionally exhibited level given the amount of eyes a film has to pass through in order to get to that stage. most online criticisms about the pacing of films is often by the easily bored and unreflective, responding to the film they wanted rather than the film it is.

but I've taught at universities for a long time and some student filmmaking groups have a way of making an 8 minute short feel like a time rift has opened up. arriving early and leaving late on everything, including all their coverage whether it adds anything or not, and throwing in a trick whip pan here or there to paper the cracks. often one of the main things that destroys rhythm is keeping focus on a weaker performer at expense of a stronger one.  

i don't think a sense of rhythm in editing is as clear as it is for, say, a drummer. Eisenstein would disagree as his notes are full of considerations of frames-as-notes (this is when rhythm came entirely from image). it's more about an instinct about when each part of a sequence has contributed as much as it needs to and no more. 

essentially, in most storytelling forms of cinema (ie. Hollywood) if you are overly noticing the pace or the hand (or absence) of the editor, there is something wrong.
  - You also see some of it in bad "B" movies where they've had to stretch the film out to get to feature-length. 

So there's a flat shot of a car driving on the street, pulling into the parking lot, parking in a parking space, then the driver gets out of the car, closes the door, walks fifty paces to the building, opens the door and walks in. No voice-over, no music, just, "the unforgettable walking-into-the-building scene from *Teenage Vampire Chainsaw Massacre 2*."
3. In all honestly, just watch a bad movie and you'll probably find bad rhythm. Scenes that go on too long, bad balance between highly-dramatic and slow scenes, the sense where you should be in a different stage of the narrative than where you are, and so on. 

I'll give you a sort of counter-example. In *The Social Network*, there was a lot of criticism that the boat racing scene sort of appears out of nowhere. Now, whether it's a good scene or not, I'd argue that this feeling is so pronounced for some because the entire rest of the film (as per Fincher generally) is so exquisitely paced. 

I think your best directors and writers really get pacing right. Take for example Soderbergh, who does films of all genres and milieus and yet somehow they always flow perfectly for what they are. *Solaris* is slow, meditative, throught-provoking, and *Out of Sight* barrels along from character to character from timeline to timeline with almost no slow moments outside of its peak - the romantic scene at the Detroit hotel - around which the film centers itself.
4. Take some time one evening and watch Ernst Lubitsch's "To be or Not to Be". You don't even have to concentrate hardly on the rhythm - it will be revealed to you. This movies is rhytmic perfection, seamlessly flowing - in every scene itself and from scene to scene, without a stutter, and it's all in service of the film, comedy and story. This movie is masterpiece and excellent example of mastery of rhythm in film.
5. Sure. I was a television editor, but the thing with editing is that when it’s done well, it’s essentially invisible. If you notice the editing, unless you’re an editor yourself, you’ve probably done something wrong.  It should enhance the story or whatever you’re trying to portray, but it should not really draw attention to itself.
6. Yes, editing is the most important aspect of filmmaking that can completely recontextualize and make or break the material. A split second too long and a scene can seem cheesy and artificial. A split second too fast and that same scene might feel awkward and more aggressive than intended.


Suicide Squad, Oppenheimer, Superman, and the first 28 Years Later are some films I consider horribly edited with a very social media-like editing philosophy. You can easily observe how they differ from a movie like Tarkovsky's Stalker or even just something like There Will Be Blood or Harakiri. The stark contrast in the rhythm shows an ADHD frenetic, disjointed, rapid fire style vs a more slowburn, contemplative one. Of course, most movies will be somewhere in between but that's just to give obvious examples how pacing can effect mood in a drastic way.

For how bad rhythm can effect dialogue, I'd recommend Signs, The Last Airbender, and Gangs of New York which all feature scenes with awkward pauses. The dialogue between Cameron Diaz and Leo particularly feels artificial here. Another great example of horrendous rhythm is Foster's Money Monster where a noticeable lack of coverage caused the film's narrative to feel directionless and meandering.

On the flipside, a movie like Muholland Drive is impeccably edited. One of those films that you watch without feeling it's runtime due to masterful pacing. Plus, the rhythm is very purposefully done to create a surrealist atmosphere, showing that awkward rhythm can be used to great effect as well.

## 5. Movies where the parts are greater than the whole - 5 Card Stud (1968)

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I was drawn to watch 5 Card Stud because the synopsis said the film was about a poker cheater who gets caught and lynched with a picture of Yaphet Kotto next to Dean Martin. The lynchers die one by one. I was curious if it was a revenge film with Dean Martin as a violent but good man and what kind of role Robert Mitchum would have. It turns out to be a somewhat standard mystery movie about Dean Martin solving the mystery even if he doesn't really care for any of the others in danger. But was this a 1968 film that directly shows a lynching of a black man in the Western setting, the same year that Night of the Living Dead came out, the same year that MLK Jr. was assassinated? 

It turns out no, that's not the case. It's a random man who gets hanged in the opening of the film. But what is evoked by a film with a lynching that ignites the plot and a black man has a supporting role? Probably best for the time period and for the type of film this is, it doesn't have anything direct to say about race. Kotto plays a bartender, but he isn't mistreated in the service role. He travels 100 miles freely to Denver to convince Dean Martin to help solve the murders of the lynchers. It's not like the film would go into the hardship a black man would encounter in 1880 for that journey. The film almost feels raceblind. Kotto as Little George, although he's referred to as just George in the film, says one thing on the topic of lynching. "I got no use for lynchers. They oughta get the same thing they give out." It's expected for the black character to die and he does so here. He fights back well, forcing the killer to change his MO to use a gun instead of strangulation. In his dying moments, George gives a final clue to assist our hero Dean Martin in who's the killer.

The film is like this with other subjects. It introduces ideas without trying to dive into them and it's not always a negative quality of films like this. The story naturally unfolds and any topic it surrounds such as mob justice, religion and violence, women in the west, evolution of small towns, etc. is emblematic of genre codes and the time it was made in. The once blacklisted writer from the HUAC, Margeurite Roberts, adds a unique touch to the writing and dialogue. The writer-director team of Roberts and Henry Hathaway will make a more iconic Western with True Grit in 1969. 

This backstory of Roberts is tempting to make a connection to anything in the film. The only thing I can quickdraw is how the lynchers and the witnesses in Kotto and Dean Cain's characters don't reveal anything to the town marshall even if it would be helpful. They keep silent to fight the threat of death themselves. 

Where Roberts feels integral is in the presentation of the female characters. We have a set up of a typical Good vs. Bad woman on the frontier. The first woman introduced feels like a typical romantic interest, but she comes across as sisterly even when kissing Dean Cain. The "Bad" woman is a sex worker, common in Westerns, however, Inger Stevens as the madame to the brothel (fronting as a barbershop where Miscellaneous services are 20 dollars) is presented as charmingly normal. Stevens as Lily Langford has witty dialogue to tease Dean Cain, but the film makes a point for the Good Nora Evers, played by Katherine Justice, to be smitten by her normalcy. Sex workers have been a major part of cinema history. They have been portrayed as hookers with hearts of gold, tragic figures, manipulative, spiteful, femme fatales, but I think Roberts did something a little different by making Lily Langford and the girls come across so mundane. The film points out the usual fashion iconography of sex workers in this setting and how it isn't always true.

Robert Mitchum is a curious new preacher in town. Dressed in black and more than eager to shoot a gun, he's a very striking character. It's not hard to figure out he's the killer, but the journey is enjoyable enough. Here we have a religious man who is avenging his brother's lynching. The deaths are justifiable in an eye for an eye philosophy, and it's not like the film doesn't want us to be on Mitchum's side, however, he must meet his comeuppance. 

The real villain of the piece is played by a very theatrical Roddy McDowall. He sticks out so much with his accent and his way of savoring every line. He isn't villainous in a bigoted or homicidal way. It's calculating. He thinks like a killer and can be impulsive but he isn't itching to kill anybody himself. He enjoys the whole predicament. You'd think he run away when the murders pile up, but he sees it as a chance to find Mitchum to do more killings for him. That's why he lies and gets George killed, out of general hate and opportunity. McDowall has quick scenes that make full use of the time, showing his inner rage when he reflects on his relationship with his father. It's interesting to watch how he is sharing good information with the rest of the crew, yet he would throw them under the bus the first chance he gets. 

There's one major action scene of the film. The miners in the town revolt against the sheriff for not doing enough about the killings. A creative shootout with decent stunts. It allows our hero to fight for the order of the community, but he isn't really a part of the town. He drifted away in the beginning and he will drift away at the end. Dean Cain plays a gambler, typical for him as an actor, but he's not a drunk. He has a good line about how he can't finish a drink if he didn't start it to put down McDowall's character.

On a conceptual level, it's easy to see how things hold the film back. There isn't a complete throughline throughout the film, no greater questions it asks. The relationship between Dean Cain and Robert Mitchum is fine. Would the film be better if it focused on a more brotherly dynamic or perhaps a stronger philosophical difference between the two to make the final confrontation more effective? The irony of Mitchum killing lynchers while trying to turn the town and Dean Cain into more pious people provides a lot of material not capitalized on. 

The film is like a proto-slasher since the kills occur without a gun. We get some creative deaths: a head suffocated in flour, strangulation with barbed wire, and a hanging in a church with the rope tied to the church bell. Mitchum as this all black, homicidal preacher naturally conjures his Night of the Hunter character. He's a commanding presence but is diminished once it's revealed who he is. The film doesn't take advantage of any secrets or use suspense for final reveals. I don't know if it was cliche then, but the gun in a hollowed out Bible he uses perfectly encapsulates the character. 

All of these subjects don't coalesce into an amazing film, but the ingredients are there. The town has character, and the film wants it to have character. The bar owner has some dialogue about a bigger bar moving in and losing regular customers. The film doesn't need to spend a lot of time on the classic Western dichotomy of civilization vs. the wild, or the civilized city vs. the small rural towns. It makes its points in small spurts. However, it feels limited in scope. 

By the late 60s, the Western film was descending in popularity even if Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid would reach number one in the box office in 1969. 5 Card Stud is an assortment of old tropes, new sensibilities, familiar star power, and hints of social discourse. It's very 1968 in how it isn't New Hollywood but isn't Old Hollywood either. It's a part of that mode of Hollywood filmmaking to make the best story you know how to make without worrying about politics or experimentation with technique and narrative. In the end, it curiously seesaws between saying something subversive and doing nothing all the while it trudges along as a well made film.

I don't think it can be reclaimed as some hidden great film, but it is one of those films where its mediocrity is interesting in itself, and it can be more useful in explaining that era of history than a lot of films that are considered better. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Five Card Stud is a film that, IMHO, had it had a little more polish to its screenplay and maybe had a different lead actor (sorry Dean) it might have made for a truly intriguing Agatha Christie-esq murder mystery set in the wild west.

To begin: I'm not a Dean Martin hater but its hard to root for him as the protagonist here.  He is ok, I guess, but it might have been more interesting to have a more stoic lead.  Perhaps a Richard Widmark in his prime or (yeah, being a little too obvious) a Clint Eastwood or Yul Brynner or Steve McQueen.

The story is told in a very episodic way, not really "tight" and seems to mosey around rather than lock in.  Robert Mitchum, it is quite clear almost from the start, is the "mysterious" avenging killer and I felt his bad guy turns in *The Night of the Hunter* and the original *Cape Fear* were more effective.  In part it was *because* he is presented from the get go as the badguy and in this film they try to muddy the waters but everyone knows he's the one who's doing the "avenging".

Which brings us to Roddy McDowall's Nick Evers... if the film lacks plenty here and there, it is McDowall's Evers that truly keeps me from calling it a total bust.  He's freaking *great* as the anarchic "burn it *all* down" bastard who doesn't care who gets run over in his thirst to upend everything.  He's an anarchist through and through and its fascinating seeing McDowell play this type of character... he's *so* unlike pretty much every other character I can think of offhand he's played through is very long career!

So, yeah, come to see Dean Martin and Robert Mitchum but stay to see some rather delicious scene chewing by McDowall!
  - Fully agree. I think Dean Martin's character is inherently flawed since the reasoning for going back after he departed from the town is basic and almost nonsensical. It doesn't come across as a tough guy wanting to face a killer or even a curious guy to see what it's all about. I'm not sure if it's the writing or Dean Martin's acting, but he's too detached from the other characters in chemistry and as part of the narrative. 

A script change for Robert Mitchum would've been a good idea since everyone is just waiting for the kills to happen. If we see it from his perspective, it adds some suspense and it would allow more interesting scenes when he's the preacher. 

I was very surprised by Roddy McDowall. I wasn't sure what age he was supposed to be since he has a young face, but he was 40 when it came out. A 40 year old weasel with no shown interest in women, and he's mostly okay with living at home with a father he dislikes? He's so idiosyncratic and strangely works in all the ways Dean Martin doesn't. It's one of the many roads the film could take. Showing this man's sad life in the west when there are strikes, myths of opportunity, and the death of hope in those arid lands.
2. The Anderson Tapes: peak-era Sean Connery as a born-loser jailbird safecracker whose Scottish accent is just accepted without any explanation whatsoever, even though the film is set in the US; Christopher Walken’s big screen debut; character actors who have maybe 2 lines but each one steals their respective scene hands down due to sheer gusto; SNL 1st year alum Garret Morris with a sizable role as a NYC cop. Also, directed by Sidney Lumet — firmly in the camp of “How in the world did this go under my radar for this many decades??!”

Amazing in many small ways yet, unless you’re a particular fan of these type of small curiosities, it would be entirely of no interest. In a way, despite their overall “midness” these are some of the best type of cinematic discoveries.
  - Nice recommendation, I see Martin Balsam is in it and he's always a good presence. Love these kinds of films.
